title: Executive Order 12853—Blocking Government of Haiti Property and Prohibiting Transactions With Haiti

flags.justification: Broad blocking authority for the Secretary of the Treasury, the standard structure for IEEPA-based sanctions orders.
No review process for affected persons is specified in this order's own text.
Multiple prohibited-transaction categories all coherently serve one purpose: sanctions against the de facto Haitian regime.

Sec. 3(c)'s catch-all anti-evasion clause and Sec. 2's Treasury-Secretary designation standard for which Haitian nationals are covered are both open-ended, standard features of sanctions-order drafting.
No incentive structure rewarding behavior contrary to the order's purpose is identifiable.
No review or reauthorization mechanism is specified.
The order is explicitly transmitted to Congress.
No external check is specified on the Secretary of the Treasury's exercise of blocking and designation authority.

Formally implements United Nations Security Council Resolution 841 into U.S. domestic law, consistent with the significant international-law connection found in EO 12724's implementation of Resolution 661.
Sec. 7 carefully manages the transition from prior orders, explicitly stating this order 'supplements' rather than replaces EO 12779 — a well-coordinated succession.
Sec. 4 narrows a prior humanitarian exemption specifically to exclude the de facto regime and blocked persons from benefiting from it — an anti-loophole-closing provision, not a broadening one.

notes: Validation batch (positive). Structural weight: 6/22 = 27.3%. Consistent with the pattern established by comparable UN-resolution-implementing sanctions orders (EO 12722, EO 12724).
